coredata.dc:title: A MELET- and IFE-based UV–visible luminescent ratiometric probe for quantization of mercury(II) and nitrofurantoin in environmental sewage
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhong-Xia
Liu, Kai-Qi
Jiang, Zhen-Tao
Meng, Xiang-Ying
Li, Feng
Wu, Ke-Chen
Li, Heng-Ye
Wang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorimetric ratiometric probe of green and eco-friendily nitrogen-enriched, oxygen-doped carbon nanodots (Cnanodots) was prepared for the quantitative analysis of mercury(II) (HgII) and nitrofurantoin (Nit) in the environmental sewage. The Cnanodots exhibits dual-emission peaks respectively at 345 and 445 nm under 285 nm excitation, with excitation-independent properties. Unexpectedly, this Cnanodots displays two obvious ratiometric responses to HgII and Nit through decreasing the signal at 345 nm and remaining invariable at 445 nm. Experimental results confirm that the highly sensitive analysis of HgII and Nit are achieved respectively based on matching energy-level electron transfer and inner filter effect mechanisms. The fluorescence (FL) ratiometric intensity of [FL345nm/FL445nm] expresses a good linear relationship with the concentration of HgII in the scope of 0.01–20 μM, while the logarithm of [Log(FL0345nm-FL345nm)] on the quenching degree of the probe by Nit also shows a good linear correlation within the range of 0.01–100 μM. The detection limits were calculated to be 4.14 nM for HgII, and 7.84 nM for Nit. Moreover, recovery experiments of Cnanodots for HgII and Nit sensing in real sewage samples obtained satisfactory results, comfirming the feasibility of practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Matching energy-level electron transfer
Inner filter effect
Ratiometric probe
Mercury(II) and nitrofurantoin analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Monitoring mechanisms of Cnanodots on HgII and Nit
Detection analysis HgII and Nit using Cnanodots as FL ratiometric probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The transmission electron microscopy image (A), FT-IR (B), full-survey XPS (C), C1s (D), N1s (E) and O1s (F) spectra of the Cnanodots. Inset (A): the size distributions of Cnanodots.

(A) FL emission (curve a), excitation (curve b) and absorption (curve c) spectra of the Cnanodots. (B) The excitation-emission correlation FL behaviours of Cnanodots under different excitation. The FL intensities respectively at 345 and 445 nm (λex, 285 nm) of the Cnanodots at different pH (C) and NaCl concentration (D).
The FL and UV–vis spectra of the Cnanodots in the non-existence and existence of different concentrations of HgII (A, B) and Nit (C, D), respectively. λex = 285 nm.

UPS spectra of Cnanodots (A) and Nit (B), (C) Optical energy gaps of Cnanodots (C) and Nit, (D) Schematic of electron transfer process from Cnanodots to Nit. (E) FL decay of the Cnanodots in the absence and presence of Nit (20 and 50 μM). (F) UV–vis (line a) and FL (line b) spectra of Cnanodots, and UV–vis spectrum of Nit (line c).

FL spectra of Cnanodots after react with various concentrations of HgII (A, top to bottom: 0–30 μM) or Nit (D, top to bottom: 0–100 μM). Linear relationships between the FL ratio signals and the CHgII (B) or CNit (E). Selectivity of the Cnanodots-based sensor for HgII (C) and Nit (F). The concentration of HgII is 30 μM; Nit, Cap, Dox, Cr3+ , Fe2+ and Fe3+ were 100 μM and that of other metal ions and biomolecules were 300 μM, respectively. λex = 285 nm.
The principle of the proposed Nit and HgII analysis concept using Cnanodots as probe.
Summarizing luminescent lifetimes and electron transfer parameters of Cnanodots in the non-existence and existence of HgII and Nit.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The average lifetime (τavg.) is obtained according to τavg. = ΣAiτi.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The FL quenching efficiency (QE) is obtained according to QE = 1 − τ0/τ.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The rate of electron transfer (kET) is obtained according to kET = 1/τ − 1/τ0. Wherein τ and τ0 respectively is the FL lifetimes of Cnanodots in the existence and non-existence of Nit or HgII.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the analysis of HgII and Nit in experimental (Em) and domestic (Ds) sewage samples, respectively (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The data were obtained from three parallel samples.

head.ce:title: A MELET- and IFE-based UV–visible luminescent ratiometric probe for quantization of mercury(II) and nitrofurantoin in environmental sewage

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorimetric ratiometric probe of green and eco-friendily nitrogen-enriched, oxygen-doped carbon nanodots (Cnanodots) was prepared for the quantitative analysis of mercury(II) (HgII) and nitrofurantoin (Nit) in the environmental sewage. The Cnanodots exhibits dual-emission peaks respectively at 345 and 445 nm under 285 nm excitation, with excitation-independent properties. Unexpectedly, this Cnanodots displays two obvious ratiometric responses to HgII and Nit through decreasing the signal at 345 nm and remaining invariable at 445 nm. Experimental results confirm that the highly sensitive analysis of HgII and Nit are achieved respectively based on matching energy-level electron transfer and inner filter effect mechanisms. The fluorescence (FL) ratiometric intensity of [FL345nm/FL445nm] expresses a good linear relationship with the concentration of HgII in the scope of 0.01–20 μM, while the logarithm of [Log(FL0345nm-FL345nm)] on the quenching degree of the probe by Nit also shows a good linear correlation within the range of 0.01–100 μM. The detection limits were calculated to be 4.14 nM for HgII, and 7.84 nM for Nit. Moreover, recovery experiments of Cnanodots for HgII and Nit sensing in real sewage samples obtained satisfactory results, comfirming the feasibility of practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion (HgII), as one of the recognized most toxic heavy metal ions (HMIs), is one of the main sources of water-pollution caused by HMIs and damage to human health [1,2]. Long-term exposure to HgII environments can lead to the accumulation and magnification of HgII in the form of the potent neurotoxin methylmercury within the food chains, eventually posing a serious threat to the ecosystem and the people's health [3,4]. Except for HMIs pollution, the antibiotics, as a type of conventional medical medication, are considered as newly emerging organic pollutants in environmental water [5]. Nitrofurantion (Nit), as a synthetic antibiotics drug, is popularly used for the prevention of gallbladder microbial bacterial infection and the treatment of urinary tract bacterial infections [6,7]. Meanwhile, Nit, for many years, has also been widely applied in husbandry to prophylaxis against and treat coccidiosis infections in farm poultry and livestock due to their high-efficiency and low-cost [8]. However, the abuse of Nit leads to its metabolites/residues into water, soil and plants through metabolism, eventually causing environmental pollution, and producing a negative impact on humans via ecological circulation. The disservices of HgII and Nit for mankind's health demands the researchers to exploit an efficient analytical method for determination of the trace amount. Among numerous analytical techniques, fluorescence sensing methods stand out due to their simplicity, robustness and high-sensitivity [9]. Wherein ratiometric method has not only all the advantages of fluorescence analysis, but also has become a research hotspot in the field of analysis in recent years due to its signal sensitivity and specificity with a high signal-to-background ratio [10,11].

Carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials (CLMs) with heteroatoms, as a newly emerging green and eco-friendly material, have attracted attention due to their simple preparation, high-yield and tunable photophysical and physicochemical properties [12–14]. Considering its tunable optical properties, the abundance of CLMs-based luminescent ratiometric probes with specific functional groups were prepared through various types of heteroatom doping, which were widely used as probes in various analytical fields [13,15–18], including environmental, medicine, food, biosensing and bioimaging. As shown in Table S1, the composition of functional group, bimodal wavelength and selectivity of probe can be easily tuned by using the regulating type or content of heteroatoms, which were benefit for the design of new CLMs-based probes of analyte in various analytical system. Noteworthily, the unicity of surface active site of the CLMs makes the multiplexed detection of the target analyte difficult. In addition, most of CLMs-based ratiometric sensing platforms are single-cycle route that cannot achieve a reset state, resulting in reducing material utilization and increasing detecting costs. Accordingly, although considerable efforts to develop CLMs-based luminescent ratiometric probes, it still remains a challenge to develop ratiometric probe with multiplexed analysis and reusability that are comparable or even superior to commercial surface modified CLMs.

Herein, a stable dual-emission nitrogen-enriched, oxygen-doped carbon nanodots (Cnanodots) was prepared employing 4-aminoantipyrine (4-Amp) as the only precursor, subsequently, as fluorimetric ratiometric probe to analyze HgII and Nit in the environmental sewage. The Cnanodots exhibits dual-emission peaks respectively at 345 and 445 nm under 285 nm excitation, with excitation-independent properties. Excitingly, as the concentration of HgII and Nit increases, the luminescent signal at 345 nm of Cnanodots gradually decrease, while the signal at 445 nm remains unchanged, signifying the capability for constructing two ratiometric sensors to measure HgII and Nit. Experimental results indicate that this sensing platform for HgII and Nit analysis are quantified based on matching energy level electron transfer (MELET) and inner filter effect (IFE) mechanisms, respectively. Moreover, recovery experiments of Cnanodots for HgII and Nit sensing in real sewage samples obtained satisfactory results. The prepared process of dual-emission Cnanodots and protocol for HgII and Nit analysis are shown in Scheme 1 .
The supplementary information provides a detailed description of the experimental details, including reagents, instruments, probe preparation and analytical procedures.

Characterization of the Cnanodots morphologies and chemical compositions helped explore their physicochemical properties. Fig. 1 A shows that the Cnanodots comprised of uniform and well-dispersed spherical nanodots with an average diameter of ∼9.0 nm (inset of the Fig. 1A). The peak at 3425 cm−1, as shown in Fig. 1B, is associated with NH/OH stretching vibration [19]. Three absorption bands at 1659, 1598 and 1509 cm−1 are respectively attributed to the CO, CN and ArCC stretching vibrations [20,21]. Such vibrational absorption signals from 750 to 1250 cm−1 are owing to CN/CO/CNH/COH vibrations [22]. Note that two bands at 3072 and 2926 cm−1 are attributed to the NH3 + stretching vibration, belongs to the protonation of Cnanodots [23]. The Ar-H (2922 cm−1) and CH2 (1441 cm−1) stretching vibrations indicate that the active groups on the Cnanodots surface are cross-linked through abundant aromatic rings and aliphatic chains [19,24]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum displays three peaks at 285.6, 399.5 and 531.6 eV, respectively belongs to the C1s, N1s and O1s regions, with the atom percentages (At%) of 73.73, 15.32 and 10.95 % (Fig. 1C). The C1s spectrum reveals the presence of five different types of carbon environments, CC (284.0 eV), CC (284.6 eV), CN (285.3 eV), CO/COH (286.2 eV) and CO/CN (287.7 eV) (Fig. 1D) [25]. The N1s spectrum shows five characteristic peaks at 398.2, 399.1, 399.9, 400.8 and 402.5 eV, which belongs to the pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, primary amino-N, secondary amino-N and oxidic-N groups (Fig. 1E), respectively [12]. The O1s spectrum was deconvoluted into four peaks at 530.9, 531.2, 532.8 and 534.9 eV, respectively belongs to CO, COH, CO and OCO groups (Fig. 1F) [12,26]. These analysis data demonstrate that this Cnanodots possesses a typical sp2/sp3-hybridized structural rigidity network with abundant polar side chains and nitrogen-enrichment, oxygen-deficiency surface active groups.

The luminescent properties of the Cnanodots were next explored. The Cnanodots exhibits a dual-peak emission located at 345 and 445 nm (Fig. 2 A, line a), under 285 nm excitation (Fig. 2A, line b). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum shows three absorption bands at ∼229, 279 and 387 nm (Fig. 2A, line c). The peak at 229 nm belongs to the π → π* electronic transition of ArCC region [13,27], while the peak at 387 nm is attributed to the CO/CN groups n → π* electronic transition originated from the Cnanodots skeleton [13]. Absorption peak at 279 nm in the middle position can be ascribed to the sp3-hybridized heteroatom-related CN and CO chromophores, determined the emission of fluorescence (FL) spectrum [28–30]. That is, the broad absorption bond at ∼279 nm can capture the high photon-energy of the excited state by surface state transitions of sp3-hybridized CN and CO groups, resulting in an UV–vis dual-emission. The excitation-emission correlation FL behaviours of Cnanodots justify that there should be two radiation channels in the carbon skeleton that do not change with the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2B). Combined with uniformity of Cnanodots distribution (Fig. 1A), this phenomenon might be due to the presence of two kinds of chromophores on the surface of Cnanodots, which can respectively effectively trapping the excited electrons of different energy levels, therewith forming both different surface radiation channels [31]. Considering that the construction of a proportional sensing platform requires the probe to possess at least both effective signals, subsequent experiments in this study were all carried out using 285 nm excitation. Meanwhile, based on the quantum yield of quinine sulfate as the standard (λex = 360 nm, 54 %), the relative quantum yield of the Cnanodots was measured and calculated to be ∼11.5 % (Fig. S1).

The stability of the probe determines its own quality as a probe. So we study the optical stability of the Cnanodots at diffirent conditions, including pH value, ionic-strength tolerance and retention time. Fig. 2C signifies that the dual-FL emission of the Cnanodots is generally stable within the broad scope of pH values (4–12), attributes to the protonation/deprotonation effect of abundant surface N/O-containing groups. And the unchange FL intensities in the concentration range of 0–300 mM of NaCl solution indicate that the Cnanodots possesses the high stability even under high ionic strength environments (Fig. 2D). Furthermore, it is worth mentioning that these Cnandots can be very stable in suspension for thirteen weeks (Fig. S2). These results evidence that our Cnandots have enormous potential for analysis applications in complex environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring mechanisms of Cnanodots on HgII and Nit
ce:section.ce:para: From above discussions, such Cnanodots might be used as a nanoprobe due to its excellent photochemical properties. Research has found that when HgII or Nit were added into the Cnanodots solution, the FL of 345 nm was gradually decreased with increasing HgII or Nit dose, while the FL of 445 nm remains almost unchanged (Fig. 3A and 3C), indicating certainly ratiometric determination for HgII and Nit using such Cnanodots. To disclose the mechanism of FL change, UV–vis analysis was first investigated. Fig. 3B shows that the adsorption peaks at ∼230 and ∼390 nm were gradually reduced as increasing HgII concentration, suggesting the presence of HgII alters the surface state and disrupts the Cnanodots skeleton [32]. The specific reason might be that HgII (4f145d10) possesses both divalent cation and empty p-orbits binding sites, making it a potential receptor for conjugated Cnanodots donator with N/O heteroatoms of lone-pair electrons [33,34]. That is, HgII interact with sp2 -hybridized aromatic surfaces and N/O-containing functional groups of Cnanodots through p-π conjugation and affinity interactions (Fig. S3), respectively, thereby occurring matching energy-level electron transfer (MELET) between Cnanodots and HgII, leading to the disappearance of adsorption peaks concurrently FL quenching at 345 nm (Fig. 3A and 3B). The results of FL lifetimes further demonstrate that HgII-induced changes in Cnanodots FL is due to the electron transfer mechanism. From Fig. S4 and Table 1 , the τavg. of the Cnanodots increases with the increase of HgII dose, illustrating the occurrence of MELET between Cnanodots and HgII [30]. The QE and kET in the Cnanodots/HgII mixed system were respectively calculated to be 16.86 % and 8 × 107 s−1 (1 μM HgII), and 39.77 % and 1.9 × 108 s−1 (10 μM HgII) by the analyzing FL lifetimes data (Table 1). And the QE value are basically consistent with steady-state quenching rate (Qs, 13.2 % at 1 μM HgII, and 44.1 % at 10 μM HgII). The Qs is achieved according to the equation of Qs = (1 − FL/FL0) × 100 %, wherein the value of FL and FL0 respectively is the FL intensity of Cnanodots at 345 nm in the existence and non-existence of HgII. Thus, a conclusion was induced that the MELET mechanism is the reason of HgII-induced FL change of Cnanodots.

For Nit sensing mechanism, almost no change was observed within the UV–vis spectra of Cnanodots after adding different concentrations of Nit (Fig. 3D), implying that the Cnanodots skeleton was not changed by Nit, and no Meisenheimer-complexes were formed [35], different from monitoring mechanisms for HgII. And this result was proved by the fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. S3).

To exclude the FL change caused by the photoelectron transfer (PET) mechanism, UV–vis and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) spectra analysis were conducted to calculate the highest occupied molecular orbital-lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (HOMO-LUMO) energy-level of the Cnanodots and Nit. The calculation of HOMO-LUMO energy-level was obtained according to the following formulas: [12,36]
(1)
E
HOMO
= -
21 .
22
-
E
cutoff
-
E
onset
eV
(2)
E
LUMO
=
E
HOMO
+
E g
eV
wherein, the Eonset and Ecutoff respectively represent the position of the onset and cutoff binding energies in the UPS spectrum. the Eg represents the absorption edge in the UV–vis spectrum. From UPS spectra, Eonset and Ecutoff of the Cnanodots were determined as 2.61 and 17.11 eV, respectively (Fig. 4 A), and Eonset and Ecutoff of the Nit were measured as 1.67 and 17.10 eV, respectively (Fig. 4B). Fig. 4C displays that the Eg values of the Cnanodots and Nit respectively is 2.51 and 2.87 eV. Thus, by formulas. (1) and (2), the EHOMO and ELUMO of the Cnanodots respectively was determined as −6.72 and −4.21 eV, and the EHOMO and ELUMO of the Nit respectively was determined as −5.79 and −2.92 eV. The Cnanodots EHOMO was more negative than the Nit EHOMO but less negative than its ELUMO , i.e., the PET between Cnanodots and Nit was not feasible (Fig. 4D). This conclusion has been confirmed by the analysis of FL lifetimes data (Fig. 4E and Table 1).

However, by analyzing the UV–vis spectrum of the Nit, the UV–vis and FL spectra of Cnanodots (Fig. 4F), we found that the Nit has a characteristic absorption peak at 345 nm, which exactly overlaps with the FL of Cnanodots at 345 nm. This finding demonstrates that the appearance of completely overlapping energy regions provides conditions for the occurrence of inner filter effect (IFE) and/or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between Cnanodots and Nit [14,37]. However, the unchange lifetimes excludes the occurrence of FRET process between Cnanodots and Nit. That is, when the presence of Nit, the energy of Cnanodots at ∼345 nm is absorbed by the Nit through IFE, resulting in FL quenching at 345 nm and maintenance at 445 nm, therewith achieving ratiometric determination for Nit. From the above results and discussion, ratiometric analysis of Cnanodots to HgII and Nit can be achieved based on two mechanisms of MELET and IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection analysis HgII and Nit using Cnanodots as FL ratiometric probe
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that Cnanodots serve as FL probe for analyzing HMIs and drug micromolecule in complex environments, the pH of the reaction system should be optimized. Fig. S5 shows that the optimal pH for detecting HgII is 4.5, both high and low pH have a negative impact on the sensitivity of the sensing platform. While the Cnanodots probe has no significant effect on analyzing Nit within the pH range of 6.0–8.0 (Fig. S6). Given that the practical application of Nit could requires implementation in physiological environments, pH 7.0 was selected as the detection pH for Nit.

To verify sensitivity of this sensing platforms, the FL response of Cnanodots toward HgII and Nit were scrutinized systematically. As shown in Fig. 5 A and 5D, as the increasing doses of HgII or Nit, the FL intensity at 345 nm gradually decreased, while the FL intensity at 445 nm remains almost unchanged. Wherein was not significantly different in the trend of change between the two FL spectra. The above FL responses indicate that Cnanodots can achieve the ratiometric FL analysis of HgII and Nit. The corresponding ratiometric intensities of [FL345nm/FL445nm] expresses a good linear relationship with the concentration of HgII in the scope of 0.01–20 μM (Fig. 5B), where the values of FL345nm and FL445nm respectively represent the FL intensity of Cnanodots at 345 and 445 nm in the existence of HgII. And the logarithm of [Log(FL0345nm-FL345nm)] on the quenching degree of the probe by Nit also shows a good linear correlation within the range of 0.01–100 μM (Fig. 5E), where the values of FL0345nm and FL345nm respectively represent the FL intensity of Cnanodots at 345 nm in the non-existence and existence of Nit. The limits of detection (LODs) were calculated to be 4.14 nM for HgII, and 7.84 nM for Nit. Note that Cnanodots-based sensing platform for HgII and Nit possesses wider linear range, lower LOD and higher accuracy, compared with other optical sensors for HgII and Nit analysis (Table S2). More importantly, the quenched FL of Cnanodots by HgII can be restored to its original state through the formation of stronger chelates between HgII and its chelator, EDTA (Fig. S7A). Significantly, the restored FL were gradually decreased again when HgII was re-added (Fig. S7B), including corresponding responses to Nit (Fig. S7C), demonstrating the Cnanodots-based HgII-sensing platform possesses good reset capability and reusability. Regrettably, we have not yet found a suitable chelating agent for Nit to restore the FL of Cnanodots quenched by Nit. Although the Cnanodots-based Nit-sensing platform cannot reset to original state, the HgII-sensing platform can perfectly achieve probe FL resetting, thereby re-detecting HgII or Nit. Therefore, it is feasible to construct two parallel proportional FL detectors Cnanodots-based to detect HgII and Nit.

Except sensitivity, selectivity is another important index of the quality of sensing platform. Then, the selectivity of Cnanodots toward HgII and Nit analysis were investigated by monitoring the FL variation in the presence of potential interferents for each itself (Ca2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Cs+, K+, Na+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ for HgII; Arg, Val, Ser, Phe, Gln, Glu, Tyr, Ala, His, Pro, Cys, GSH, Cap and Dox for Nit) (Figs. S8 and S9). Note that among respective various potential interferents, only HgII and Nit can cause significant emission decrease at 345 nm, and maintain unchanged at 445 nm emission, while other interferents had no significant influence on the FL intensity (Fig. 5C and 5F). These results manifest that the Cnanodots-based sensing probe can be rendered as multivariate probes for the detection of HgII and Nit with high sensitivity and selectivity, and had the potential to be applied in real sample analysis.

The above data approve the potentiality of our constructed sensing platforms to analyze HgII and Nit in real samples. To validate the potential universality, we used the traditional spiked recovery method to monitor HgII and Nit in sewage samples. The detection results are listed in Table 2 . The recoveries for HgII detection were in the range of 96.0–105.0 % with satisfactory RSD (<5%), and the desirable quantitative recoveries for Nit were also achieved from 98.0 to 105.5 % with desirable RSD (<5%). The analysis results of actual samples confirm that the Cnanodots nanoprobe is reliable and practical for HgII and Nit detection in their respective complex environments.

In short, we have successfully synthetized a novel nitrogen-enriched, oxygen-doped Cnanodots by a simple one-pot solvothermal path. The energy-level coordination between HgII and sp2-hybridized aromatic surface or N/O-containing groups emerges the MELET process, which results in downgrade of FL intensity at 345 nm, and keep stable of the emission at 445 nm simultaneously. And the same phenomenon applies to Nit as well, but the change of FL is due to the IFE mechanism for Cnanodots to Nit. Notably, the ratiometric FL intensity is CHg II- or CNit-dependent and presents two good linear relationships in the ranges of 0.01–20 and 0.01–100 μM for HgII and Nit, respectively. And the LODs of our constructed sensing platform is not higher or even lower than many reported optical-based sensing platforms. Given the ratiometric detection application of the Cnanodots for HMIs and drug, this study inspires in-depth design, and broaden application pathway of carbon-based probe.

Zhong-Xia Wang: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Kai-Qi Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Zhen-Tao Jiang: Investigation, Data curation. Xiang-Ying Meng: Validation. Feng Li: Visualization. Ke-Chen Wu: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Heng-Ye Li: Methodology. Wei Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Project of State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2309), the Jiangsu Province for the College Graduate Research and Innovation Projects (KYCX23_3470), and the Fujian Key Laboratory of Advanced Marine Materials, Minjiang University (MJUKF-FMSM202206).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124805.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Atmospheric mercury isotope shifts in response to mercury emissions from underground coal fires

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Sun, F. Cao, S. Dai, B. Shan, C. Qi, Z. Xu, P. Li, Y. Liu, W. Zheng, J. Chen, Atmospheric mercury isotope shifts in response to mercury emissions from underground coal fires, Environ. Sci. Technol. 57 (2023) 8638-8649.
